item.title: Canada clears NATO's 2% bar — after years of lagging and a last-minute lift
item.description: Canada has finally met NATO’s two per cent defence spending target, buoyed by a $9.3 billion surge and internal accounting changes. The long-elusive milestone fulfills Prime Minister Mark Carney’s promise under intense allied pressure, but comes as NATO shifts the goalposts farther and scrutiny turns to how the money is actually spent.

item.title: Shopify flexes its political muscle with Canadian lobbying blitz
item.description: The post Shopify flexes its political muscle with Canadian lobbying blitz appeared first on The Logic .

item.title: CP NewsAlert: NATO says Canada met 2% spending pledge
item.description: OTTAWA - NATO says Canada has finally met a key alliance commitment by spending roughly two per cent of its GDP on defence in the last fiscal …

item.title: Provinces and Ottawa square off over notwithstanding clause at Supreme Court
Toronto residents will be heard on Billy Bishop airport, Liberal MPs say, as city council looks at legal options
Anti-hate bill that provoked bitter debate over religious freedom passes the Commons
item.description: Bill C-9 will now move to the Senate

item.title: Police Federation head wants risk assessment for officers collecting firearms under Ottawa’s buyback scheme
item.description: Brian Sauvé warns that loading of assault-style weapons in cars, en route to hand them in, may prompt 911 calls and tactical units being called in

item.title: Leaderless corporate watchdog remains ‘important,’ Ottawa looking for executive, Anand says
item.description: Canadian Ombudsperson for Responsible Enterprise hasn’t had a permanent head for almost two years

item.title: Contentious anti-hate legislation passes final vote in the House, now moves to Senate
item.description: The Liberal government's politically contentious new anti-hate bill has passed its final hurdle in the House of Commons and will now go to the Senate.

item.title: Quebec’s Bill 21 is being heard before the Supreme Court. Here’s what you need to know
item.description: Quebec’s secularism law is before the court, with debates over religious freedoms and the province’s use of the notwithstanding clause

item.title: RCMP commissioner regrets Indigenous spying program that spanned over a decade
item.description: The RCMP commissioner says he expresses sincere regret for an extensive spying program that targeted hundreds of Indigenous people following a CBC Indigenous investigation.

item.title: Politics Insider: Air Canada CEO under heavy criticism for English-only condolence message
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney said the airline has a responsibility to communicate in both official languages

item.title: National divide emerges over notwithstanding clause at Supreme Court hearings on Bill 21
item.description: Legal arguments at the Supreme Court challenge into Quebec’s secularism law Wednesday focused on how provinces use the notwithstanding clause, exposing a national divide. Some provinces agree with Quebec’s approach while others — and Ottawa — see a need for more limits.

item.title: Doug Ford warns Bonnie Crombie he will ‘send an army’ to stop her becoming Mississauga mayor
Veterans Affairs minister announces ‘independent review’ of rehab program that former soldiers say has failed them
With hindsight, former immigration minister says he would have capped international students sooner
item.description: Justice Minister Sean Fraser, who was in charge of immigration during some of the years Auditor General Karen Hogan found instances of fraud in Canada's international student program, said with hindsight, he would have acted sooner to fundamentally change it. The Opposition Conservatives have been calling for his resignation, along with his successor and the current immigration minister.

item.title: Privacy commissioner challenges Doug Ford’s reasons for changing access-to-information rules
item.description: Information and privacy commissioner Patricia Kosseim has filed a six-point rebuttal to Premier Doug Ford's "various" reasons for exempting hi…

item.title: Gun control group criticizes Ottawa over ‘poor participation’ in firearm buyback
item.description: OTTAWA - Gun control advocacy group PolySeSouvient blames "weak political leadership" for what it calls "poor participation" in a federal comp…

item.title: First Nations call for audit of Human Rights Commission following death of 3-year-old
Mark Carney slams Air Canada CEO for English-only message after pilots die in crash
Alberta, Ottawa reach 'agreement-in-principle' on methane emissions
item.description: Alberta will be in control of regulating its methane emissions under an agreement-in-principle with Ottawa.

item.title: Doug Ford and Mark Carney’s HST rebate on new homes will cost $2.2B
Public safety minister calls report of RCMP spying on Indigenous leaders ‘deeply troubling’
Should voting be mandatory in Canada? The NDP plan to make it an issue at their convention
Ford government warns school boards to keep graduation ceremonies ‘apolitical’
Almost half of former NDP voters don’t recognize names of leadership candidates: poll
House committee calls for changes to Elections Act to thwart long ballot protests
item.description: The Longest Ballot Committee has targeted ridings in a number of byelections dating back to 2022 and has said it is signing up candidates in t…

item.title: Carney 'very disappointed' in Air Canada CEO's English-only condolence video, says it lacked compassion
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney had pointed words for the head of Canada's largest airline early Wednesday, saying the Air Canada CEO's unilingual message of condolences to those affected by the recent deadly crash showed a "lack of judgment" and "a lack of compassion."

item.title: Doug Ford says he would handily win re-election as he defends keeping cellphone records secret
Canada is not planning to reopen embassy in Syria
Global Affairs laying off its most skilled diplomats, union says
Mark Carney invokes Christian values as top court told religious beliefs don’t belong in government
Doug Ford accuses privacy commissioner of politicizing his changes to access to information law
item.description: Premier Doug Ford is lashing out at Ontario's privacy commissioner, accusing her of politicizing his decision to retroactively exempt himself …

item.title: Ford government planning to waive HST on new homes for 1 year
item.description: Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy is expected to announce that the provincial portion of the harmonized sales tax will be removed for anyone buying a newly constructed home.

item.title: Sponsored international travel by MPs drops to 15-year low outside of COVID-19 pandemic
item.description: Members of Parliament last year went on the fewest sponsored international trips in the last 15 years, excluding the height of the COVID-19 pandemic.

item.title: Canada is pitching its energy ambitions in Texas — can the oil and gas industry be convinced?
item.description: Though oil and gas industry executives at CERAWeek, a global energy conference in Houston, welcomed Natural Resources Minister Tim Hodgson’s enthusiasm, they still say they want to see considerable action before they can be sure Canada's federal government is serious about changing the country’s reputation as being reluctant to expand the energy sector, especially oil and natural gas.

item.title: The NDP ponders a leap with Avi Lewis
item.description: Their big wave came with practical steps, but bruised New Democrats may be in a mood for social democratic dreams

item.title: Ottawa rejects bid to prevent sharing information about foreign-born Canadians with provinces, other countries
item.description: The federal government notified the Senate that it will not agree to amendment to Bill C-12, the immigration and asylum bill

item.title: Ottawa disbands centralized task force seeking justice for victims of plane shot down by Iran
item.description: Global Affairs says work is still being done, just not by one centralized group

item.title: UN committee criticizes Canada for not filling corporate human rights abuses watchdog role
item.description: Carney government has left the top ombudsman position vacant for 10 months

item.title: Liberals to debate minimum age for social media, AI chatbots at national convention
item.description: Age restrictions on using social media and AI chatbots are among the topics up for debate when the Liberal grassroots gathers next month for the party's national convention.

item.title: Minister pressed why just 1 Iranian official deported after 24 deemed part of terror group
item.description: Public Safety Minister Gary Anandasangaree faced pointed questions Tuesday about why the federal government has deported one Iranian official, despite longstanding concerns about how the regime operates in Canada and abroad.

item.title: Air Canada CEO summoned to Ottawa over English-only condolence video for LaGuardia crash
item.description: Air Canada's CEO has been summoned to Ottawa to explain why he spoke only one of the nation's official languages when he offered condolences for those killed and hurt in the collision at New York's LaGuardia Airport on Sunday.

item.title: India proposes trusted traveller program to ease access to Canada for Indian businesspeople
item.description: NEXUS-type program would also benefit business travellers from Canada who are seeking swifter access to India

item.title: Politics Insider: Panel finds abuse at all levels of Canadian sport
item.description: Canadian sport system is in urgent need of a fundamental overhaul, Future of Sport in Canada Commission says

item.title: Ford says privacy commissioner opposing record restrictions ‘politically driven’
item.description: TORONTO - Ontario Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday he believes the information and privacy commissioner, who is speaking out about changes he is…

item.title: Montreal-area byelection to use write-in ballots due to dozens of protest candidates
item.description: The head of Elections Canada said voters will have to write in their pick in next month's byelection in an off-island Montreal suburb due to the long list of protest candidates running in the riding.

item.title: Trim OAS for higher income seniors? 73% says yes, new poll suggests
item.description: A new poll finds there is growing support for a proposal to lower an Old Age Security (OAS) threshold for some Canadians in order to help reduce the federal government's deficit.

item.title: Canada discusses Keystone XL revival with Trump administration officials
item.description: Canadian and American government officials discussed a proposed revival of the canceled Keystone XL oil pipeline during a meeting in Houston, Tx. this week.

item.title: What’s at stake as Supreme Court hears challenge to Quebec’s Bill 21
item.description: From the use of the notwithstanding clause to state secularism to Quebec's unique place within Canada, constitutional law experts say the case will result in a major new precedent.

item.title: Quebec government within its rights to adopt Bill 21, lawyer argues
item.description: Lawyers for the Quebec government argued in Supreme Court today that the province was within its rights when it adopted its secularism law, known as Bill 21.

item.title: Justice minister won't meet premiers' demand for 'sea change' to how judges are appointed
item.description: Justice Minister Sean Fraser says he has no plans to change how judges are appointed in Canada after four premiers wrote to Prime Minister Mark Carney asking for more of a say in the process.

item.title: Federal justice minister rejects premiers’ appeal for input on judicial appointments
item.description: Premiers of Ontario, Quebec, Saskatchewan and Alberta wrote to the Prime Minister with the request

item.title: To stop long ballot protests, MP committee calls on government to change candidacy rules
item.description: A House of Commons committee is calling on the government to make changes to federal election rules it says would make it harder for electoral reform advocates to stage long ballot protests.

item.title: Federal minimum wage increasing to $18.15 per hour
item.description: The federal minimum wage will rise by 40 cents next week, a scheduled increase that keeps the wage in line with inflation.

item.title: 'Fragmented, inconsistent, unsafe': Commission calls for sweeping change to sport in Canada
item.description: A commission studying the future of sport in Canada has called for sweeping change to the way sport is structured, after its work found “the widespread presence of maltreatment and abuse” in a poorly resourced and organized system.

item.title: Progress on Phoenix pay system backlog is ‘limited,’ auditor general finds
item.description: The federal government is running out of time to clear the backlog of public service pay transactions under the old Phoenix system, said Canada's auditor general.

item.title: Canadians with ‘Havana syndrome’ outraged as Ottawa backs disputed report
item.description: Global News spoke with eight former diplomats who say they and their family members — including children — continue to suffer mysterious symptoms after incidence in Cuba years ago.

item.title: Manitoba government expands tax exemption on food, aims to cut deficit in new budget
item.description: Provincial sales tax will be removed from food and beverages sold in grocery stores on July 1

item.title: Doug Ford takes over Billy Bishop as Ottawa stays on sidelines amid Toronto pushback
How RCMP spies infiltrated the 1970s Indigenous rights movement
item.description: A CBC Indigenous investigation has found the Cold War-era RCMP Security Service had a widespread and intrusive surveillance operation targeting at least 30 legitimate Indigenous political organizations and hundreds of Indigenous people.

item.title: Future of once-heralded corporate watchdog remains under review, Ottawa says
item.description: Carney government won’t say whether budget cuts hit departments overseeing forced-labour laws

item.title: Ottawa’s foreign student cuts went far deeper than planned
item.description: The post Ottawa’s foreign student cuts went far deeper than planned appeared first on The Logic .

item.title: Canada’s international student program lacks crucial controls: audit
item.description: Auditor General Karen Hogan says there are critical weaknesses in the integrity controls for the International Student Program.

item.title: Doug Ford defends FOI changes, warns of enemies seeking government secrets
Auditor-General flags weaknesses in IRCC’s anti-fraud controls for international student program
item.description: Department failed to investigate more than 145,000 students flagged as not complying with the terms of their permits, report says

item.title: What’s at stake in 3 upcoming byelections for the House of Commons
item.description: Three byelections are being held on April 13 and the results could have an impact on both the makeup of Parliament and how long it lasts.

item.title: House of Commons returns as Iran war continues and audit reports due
item.description: Members of Parliament are back in the House of Commons today, where the government is expected to face tough questions about new auditor general reports and the war in Iran.
